content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Chairman Davidian                                    Date : September 17, 1991

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

Subject : Issues Concerning Gifts

     At your request the Legal Division has analyzed four general
issues concerning gifts to public officials.  We understand that
these issues have been raised by some members of the Legislature.
Each issue, and its respective analysis, is set forth below.

                              ISSUE 1

Has an official received a gift when a gift is given to the
official's spouse or immediate family member?  If the official has
received a gift, what is its value?

     Current Rule: Commission Regulation 18726.2 provides the
existing rule regarding these types of gifts.  It addresses two
situations:

     (1)  The gift is given directly to a member of the official's
immediate family.

     Generally, in this situation there is no gift to the official
unless he or she uses the gift, disposes of the gift or enjoys direct
benefit from the gift.  If this occurs, the official has
received the gift and the full value is attributed to the
official.  By advice letter, the Commission has created an
exception to this rule where the gift is given to the official's
spouse and the circumstances show that the donor in no way
intended to benefit the official.  In that situation, there is no
gift to the official even though he or she may derive some benefit
from the gift.

     Example:  Old family friend of the official and his spouse
sends a letter to the spouse saying:  "Enclosed are the keys to my
mobilehome.  Please feel free to use it any time."  If the
official and spouse use the mobilehome, it is a gift in its
entirety to the official based on the reasonable value of the use.

     Example:  Same situation, but the gift is from the spouse's
employer, who does not know the official and is offering use of

Chairman Davidian
Page 2


the mobilehome as a reward for the spouse's work at the office.
This is not a gift to the official even if the official uses the
mobilehome.

     (2)  The gift is given to the official and designated for use
by the official and his or her spouse or family.

     Here, there is a gift in its entirety to the official only
where he or she exercises control over who actually receives the
gift or if he or she receives a direct benefit from the gift.

     Example:  Old family friend gives official an authentic
replica of the Liberty Bell on the Fourth of July, saying, "Here's
a little gift for you and your wife." This would be a gift in its
entirety to the official because he is receiving a direct benefit
from the gift.

     Example:  Corporation sends official a letter with two
airline tickets and hotel reservations for two enclosed. The
letter reads: "Dear Official: Here are airline tickets and hotel
reservations for you and your spouse to spend one night at our
annual convention in Cooperstown, N.Y. You have agreed to address
our convention on the value of professional baseball to the
California economy." The cost of one airline ticket and one-half
of the hotel reservation are gifts to the official. The cost of
the spouse's ticket and reservation only become gifts to the
official if he chooses, for example, to bring his niece or some
other person to the convention.

     Concern:  As written, Regulation 18726.2 is not as clear as
it could be and does not contain all of the Commission's advice on
this subject. Furthermore, the regulation creates a problem for
the donor when he makes a gift directly to the official's family
member and the official directly benefits from the gift. How and
when is the donor able to ascertain that the official has
benefited from the gift and has thereby made a gift to the
official? Also, as in the Example of the mobilehome above, how
will the donor know the value of the gift?

     Proposed Changes to the Regulation:  Staff's proposed
amendments to Regulation 18726.2 do not seek to significantly
alter the current rules on this subject. Rather, they seek to
clarify the existing rules and ensure that they are all contained
in the regulation. However, in addressing the issues of the
donor's knowledge outlined immediately above, it may be necessary
to slightly alter the rule on when a gift given to the official's
spouse or family member becomes a gift to the official.

     Specifically, staff is considering an option that would
modify the regulation to state that a gift given directly to and
designated for the official's spouse or family member is a gift in
its entirety to the official unless the gift is such that the
official cannot directly benefit from it, or the circumstances

Chairman Davidian
'Page 3


indicate that the donor in no way intended to benefit the
official. However, the proposed regulation will include an
exception for gifts of meals or tickets, which will be considered
separate gifts to the official and his or her family member as
long as the family member is the person who uses that gift.

Conclusion: This is an extremely difficult subject in which
to make regulations which are absolutely fair in every case and at
the same time prevent abuses of the law. Staff has continuously
sought comments from officials and their representatives so that
the regulations can be made to apply in a practical and
understandable manner. We continue to encourage comments so that
they may be considered and possibly incorporated into the
regulation before it is presented to the Commission for adoption.

ISSUE 2

When an official receives a gift from an organization, who is
considered to be the source of the gift?

Current Rule: When an official receives a gift from an
organization, the organization is the source of the gift and the
gift limit is applied to the total value of the gift provided to
the official by the organization. However, an exception exists.
If the organization is acting as an intermediary, it will not be
considered the "source" of the gift to the public official. Under
such circumstances, the organization will be reported as an
intermediary if the gift is $50 or more, and the gift limit will
be applied against the actual source of the gift. (Section 87210.)

Prior Advice: Generally our prior advice has not focused
upon whether an organization is the source of a gift or merely an
intermediary, except in a limited way with respect to the
reporting of activity expenses by lobbyist employers. (See, Bell
Advice Letter, No. I-91-077.) We have advised that members of an
association are generally not considered sources of gifts made by
the association. (Nawi Advice Letter, No. A-84-Q13; Brockman
Advice Letter, No. I-91-096.) However, we have also advised that
members of an organization may be considered the source of a gift
if the sole or primary purpose of their dues payment is to make
gifts to public officials. (Harper Advice Letter, No. A-87-088;
Brockman Advice Letter, supra.) We have advised that a nonprofit
foundation could be considered an intermediary if it were merely
acting as a conduit for donations received and then given to
public officials. (Katz Advice Letter, No. I-90-269.) We have
recently advised that a person making a donation to an
organization may be considered the source of a gift to a public
official if, at the time the payment is made, the person knows or
had reason to know that the official was the intended recipient.
(McCloskey Advice Letter, No. I-91-263.)

Chairman Davidian
.Page 4


Concern: For purposes of the gift and honoraria provisions
of the Act, the term "source" should be interpreted in a manner
consistent with the declarations and purposes of the Act. Public
officials, whether elected or appointed, should perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own
financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have
supported them. (Government Code Section 81001.) Assets and
income of public officials which may be materially affected by
their official actions should be disclosed. (Government Code
Section 81002.)

Therefore, it is important that an organization not be deemed
to be the "source" of a gift when it is actually serving as an
intermediary. To do so would result in incomplete disclosure to
the public and would prevent the public from making an accurate
assessment of possible sources of bias on the part of a public
official.

Furthermore, to fail to recognize the actual source of a gift
could permit a person to circumvent the gift limits by making a
gift to a public official through an organization. For example,
gifts of travel are generally subject to the new gift limits.
(Government Code Section 89506(b).) However, gifts of travel from
a charitable organization are exempt from the limits if the travel
is reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose or
to an issue of state, national or international public policy.
(Government Code Section 89506(a)(2).)

If an individual whose gift of travel would be subject to the
limits were to make that gift through a charitable organization,
the individual would be able to circumvent the gift limit unless
he or she were recognized as the true source of the gift. If,
instead, the charitable organization is recognized as an
intermediary, both the charitable organization and the actual
donor will be disclosed.

Proposed Regulations 18945 and 18945.3: Staff is proposing
two regulations focusing on when a person is to be considered the
source of a gift and when that person should be considered an
intermediary.

(1) Proposed regulation 18945 provides that a person may be
considered the direct or indirect source of a gift. Thus, a person is the
source of a gift if that person tenders a gift directly to a
public official and the public official accepts the gift. A
person would also be considered the source of a gift if the person
tenders a payment to a third party, for example an organization,
and knows or has reason to know at the time the payment is made
that the payment will be used to make a gift to a public official
or to an identifiable group of public officials. Dues payments to
an organization would not be considered indirect gifts to persons
who receive gifts from the organization paid from those dues.

Chairman Davidian
Page 5


unless the sole or primary purpose of the dues payments was to
make gifts to public officials.

(2) Proposed regulation 18945.1 clarifies that a person, for
example an organization, which receives a payment from another for
the purpose of making a gift to a public official or to an
identifiable group of public officials is an intermediary. As an
intermediary, the organization would be required to disclose to
the donee not only the organization's name, address and business
activity, if any, but also the name and business activity, if any, of the actual source of the gift. In order to
avoid any misunderstanding or surprise on the part of the donee,
the proposed regulation requires that the intermediary disclose
this information at the time the gift is made.

Examples: In most situations, this determination of the
source of a gift is easy. Generally, the "source" of a gift is
going to be the person who tenders the gift to the public
official. Thus, if John Doe tenders a gift to public official Y
and Y accepts, John Doe is the "source" of a gift to Y. If ABC
charitable organization tenders a gift to official Y and Y
accepts, ABC is the "source" of a gift to Y.

Both of the foregoing examples assume that no third party was
involved in the gift. That is, the presumption is that the gift
to official Y was paid for by John Doe or ABC charitable
organization. If this is not the case, John Doe or ABC charitable
trust may not be the source of the funds. Consider the
following:¹

1. Suppose that Mary Smith has asked John Doe to make a gift
to official Y and provides funds to John Doe for that purpose. If
John Doe then uses the funds to make a gift to official Y, who
should be considered the source of the gift to official Y?

Mary Smith should be considered the source of the gift. She
knew or had reason to know at the time she made the payment to
John Doe that the payment would be used to make a gift to official
Y. John Doe should inform official Y, at the time of the gift,
that Doe is an intermediary and that Smith is the source.

2. Suppose that ABC charitable organization approaches Mary
Smith and says that ABC is planning to send official Y on a fact-
finding trip to Europe. ABC then asks Mary Smith to make a
donation to ABC to defray all or part of the costs of the trip.
Who should be considered the source of the gift to official Y?

_______________________
¹ The following examples assume that the public official receives
a gift. In many circumstances, certain payments may be income
rather than gifts, may be excluded from the definition of "gift,"
or may be exempt from the gift limits by other provisions of the
Act or by Commission regulation.

.Chairman Davidian
Page 6


Mary Smith should be considered the source of all or a
proportionate part of the gift to official Y. She knew or had
reason to know at the time she made the payment to ABC that the
payment would be used to make a gift to official Y. ABC should
inform official Y, at the time of the gift, that ABC is an
intermediary and that Smith is the source of the gift.

3. Would the analysis change if Mary Smith approached ABC
charitable organization with the plan to finance official Y's trip
and then made a donation to ABC organization towards those costs?

The analysis would be the same.

4. Suppose that ABC charitable organization approaches Mary
Smith and says that ABC is planning to send a group of interested
citizens on a fact-finding trip to Europe. If Mary Smith makes a
donation to ABC organization to defray all or part of the costs of
the trip, and one of the participants turns out to be official Y,
who is the source of the gift to official Y?

ABC would be considered the source of the gift to official Y.
At the time that Mary Smith made a donation to ABC, she did not
know or have reason to know that official Y would be a donee.

5. Suppose that ABC charitable organization decides to put
on a golf tournament and invite official Y. ABC approaches Mary
Smith and asks for donations to pay for the event. If Mary Smith
knows or has reason to know that official Y is an invitee, will
Mary Smith be considered a source of all or some pro rata portion
of gifts received by official Y from ABC charitable organization?

If Mary Smith knows or has reason to know that her donation
towards the event will be used to make a gift to official Y, Mary
Smith would be considered a source of the gift to official Y, and
ABC would be considered an intermediary. ABC should inform
official Y, at the time of the gift, that Mary Smith is the donor
and that ABC is the intermediary.

Conclusion: The proposed regulations provide a workable
framework for assuring full and adequate disclosure of sources of
gifts to public officials and for limiting the opportunities for
circumvention of the gift limits. As a general rule, the source
of a gift will be the person who tenders the gift directly to the
public official. Only in certain narrow circumstances, determined
at the time the initial payment is tendered, will a person be
considered the indirect source of a gift. Public officials who

Chairman Davidian
Page 7


receive gifts are protected from undue surprise as to the source
of the gift by the requirement for intermediary disclosure at the
time the gift is made, by which the official must be apprised of
the true source of the gift.

ISSUE 3

When must an official cumulate the value of a gift given to
him by an individual with the pro rata value of a gift given by a
group to which the individual has contributed?

Current Rule: An official must cumulate the value of a gift
given to him by an individual with the pro rata value of a gift
given by a group to which the individual has contributed either
when the group is directed or controlled by the individual or when
the group uses funds for the gift that are specifically
contributed for the purpose of making such gifts to the official.
A few examples will illustrate this rule.

Example 1: Official A receives a gift (Gift 1) from B in
1991. B then joins with C, D, and E in the same calendar year to
give another gift (Gift 2) to A, and the gift is identified as
being from B, C, D, and E. Under these circumstances A must
cumulate the value of Gift 1 with B's pro rata share of Gift 2.

Example 2: Official A receives a gift (Gift 1) from B in
1991. B then joins with C, D, and E, from a group called "Good
Government People," and the group collects funds from B, C, D, and
E to give a gift (Gift 2) to A in the same calendar year. Since
the "Good Government People" collected funds specifically to make
a gift to A, A must cumulate the value of Gift 1 with B's pro rata
share of Gift 2. Government Code Section 87210 requires that an
intermediary making a gift to an official disclose both his own
full name, street address, and business activity if any, and the
full name, street address, and business activity if any of the
actual donor. Under the above circumstances if "Good Government
People" does not disclose that Gift 2 is from B, C, D, and E, with
A is
under no obligation to cumulate B's pro rata share of Gift 2 with
Gift 1. However, if A knew, or should have known that "Good
Government People" consisted of B and some other persons, or that
B was connected to "Good Government People," A would have an
obligation to inquire whether Gift 2 was attributed in whole, or
in part, to B.

If A discovers at some future date that Gift 2 was
attributable to B, A must determine the pro rata share of Gift 2
attributable to B and cumulate it with the value of Gift 1. If this
cumulative value exceeds $250, A will not be deemed to have
violated the gift limits if A returns the portion of the value of
the gift in excess of $250 within 30 days of the date that A
discovers that Gift 2 was attributable to B. (Regulation 18726.1;
Duffy Advice Letter, No. A-84-84.)

Chairman Davidian
Page 8


Example 3: Official A receives a gift (Gift 1) from B in
1991. B is a wine producer and is a member of the "California
Wine Institute" (the "Wine Institute"). The Wine Institute gives
a gift (Gift 2) to A in the same calendar year.

Under these circumstances, the gift is from the Wine
Institute and A does not have to cumulate the value of Gift 1 with
B's pro rata share of Gift 2 merely because B is a member of the
Wine Institute. If B and some other members of the Wine Institute
specifically provided funds for purposes of making gift 2 to A,
then Wine Institute would be an intermediary and would be required
to disclose to A in addition to its own name, street, business
activity, but also the names, street address, and business
activities of the individual contributors to Gift 2. A must then
cumulate the value of Gift 1 with B's pro rata share of Gift 2.

Concerns: Concerns have been raised that an official who
receives a gift from a group may not be aware that a member of the
group has previously given a gift as an individual and the
official may exceed the gift limits imposed by the Act and may
thereby inadvertently violate the law. However, as the above
examples indicate, the official must cumulate when the official
knows, or should have known that a gift from a group is
attributable, in whole or in part, to an individual who has
previously given a gift. If the official determines at a later
date that a gift from a group was in fact attributable in part to
an individual who had previously given a gift and the cumulative
value of gifts from that individual exceeded the limits, the
official should, within 30 days of the date he or she discovers
this fact, return to or reimburse, the individual for that portion
of the gift in excess of the limits.

Conclusion: Because of the question of knowledge of the
official, this issue does not lend itself to an easy solution by
regulation. However, staff is prepared to work with legislative
staff to address any perceived problems.

ISSUE 4

What types of expenditures are "directly related to a
political purpose" under the Act's "personal use" provisions?

Background: Prior to 1990, the "personal use" law was in the
Elections Code and under the aegis of the Attorney General. At
that time, and now, the basis for determining the permissibility
of an expenditure of campaign funds is the degree of relation
between the expenditure and a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. HOWEVER, when the Legislature incorporated
personal use into the Political Reform Act (as of January 1,
1990), it also significantly altered the applicable standards for
the permissible expenditure of campaign funds, thereby
substantially raising the acceptable thresholds.

.Chairman Davidian
Page 9


     Specifically, prior law allowed an expenditure of campaign
funds that had "more than a negligible political, legislative, or
governmental purpose" even when personal benefit accrued to an
elected officer from the expenditure. Expenditures in certain
categories were required to meet a higher standard, that of a
"reasonable relation" to the required purposes.

     Current Rule: The current law, raised the basic standard for
expenditure of campaign funds from the lower "more than a
negligible relation" standard that constituted prior law to the
level of the former upper tier, the "reasonably related" level.
Further, where a candidate or elected officer receives a
substantial personal benefit (defined as a direct personal benefit
valued at over $100) from the expenditure of campaign funds, a new
and significantly narrower standard, the "directly related"
standard, has been expressly mandated by the legislature.

     In other words, expenditures of campaign funds must now, at a
minimum, be "reasonably related" to a political, legislative or
governmental purpose. And if the expenditure confers a direct
personal benefit of over $100 on the elected officer or candidate,
that expenditure must be "directly related" to a political,
legislative or legislative purpose. Also, it is important to note
that several categories of campaign expenditures, such as
purchases of tickets to sporting events and personal gifts, are
required to satisfy the "directly related" standard, regardless of
whether personal benefit is obtained. (See Government Code
Section 89513.)

     Proposed Regulation: The definitional language proposed in
new Regulation 18952 for "directly related to a political purpose"
requires that an expenditure of campaign funds be "necessary,"
"inherent," or "inextricably linked" to a political purpose.
These are narrow parameters, to be sure, but the terms do reflect
the alterations enacted by the Legislature. Staff recognizes,
after several months of grappling with the issue of personal use
definitions, that terms such as "directly related" and "reasonably
related" do not lend themselves to precise definition; but it was
felt that the terms are so basic to the personal use statutes that
it was important to give guidance regarding the meaning of the
phrases on which the entire framework of personal use is based.

     The proposed definitional regulation recognizes that certain
activities, such as fundraising and activities to inform voters of
the record and viewpoints of a candidate, are fundamental to the
political process. Consequently, a list of activities, deemed to
be "directly related to a political purpose" is included, for
purposes of practicality, in the proposed regulation. If a
candidate were to expend funds for any of the listed activities
which encompass the political campaign activity spectrum, there
would be no need to justify the expenditure as "directly related."

Chairman Davidian
Page 10


Conclusion: A copy of the proposed regulation is enclosed
for your review; I think a reading of the proposed regulatory
language will indicate that the list of directly related
activities is workable, in that it incorporates necessary campaign
activities while taking into consideration the current
legislatively-mandated statutory strictures.

SH:aa:gifts1

Adopt 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18952 to read:

18952.    Definitions of Directly Related to a Political Purpose,
Reasonably Related to a Political Purpose, Directly
Related to a Legislative or Governmental Purpose, and
Reasonably Related to a Legislative or Governmental
Purpose

The following definitions shall pertain to any

expenditure of campaign funds by a candidate or elected officer

pursuant to Government Code Sections 89510-89521.

     (a) Directly related to a political purpose. [An

expenditure of campaign funds by a candidate or a candidate

controlled committee is directly related to a political purpose if

the expenditure is [necessary]* [inherent]* [inextricably linked]*

to the candidate's quest for elective office.]* Expenditures

deemed to be directly related to a political purpose include [but

are not limited to]*:

     (1) Expenditures in connection with fundraising for the

candidate's campaign for elective office.

     (2) Expenditures for meals provided to the candidate,

campaign staff members, and contributors at meetings, the [sole]*

purpose of which is to discuss campaign issues or strategy.

     (3) Expenditures in connection with programs or events

to inform voters as to [the past record or current position]*

[views]* of the candidate on campaign issues.


___________________________

* The text appearing in brackets represents a choice which the
Commission may wish to consider. The Commission may select the
choice, exclude the choice, or consider other choices.

8/29/91                    1                         18952

(4)     Expenses for get-out-the-vote promotional mailings

and get-out-the-vote events in the candidate's jurisdiction.

     (5)  Expenses incurred for operation of the candidate's

controlled committee [in connection with performance of the

administrative functions of the committee].*

     (b) Reasonably related to a political purpose.  An

expenditure of campaign funds is reasonably related to a political

purpose when it is [reasonably]* foreseeable that the expenditure

would further the quest for elective office of the candidate

expending the funds.

     (c) Directly related to a legislative or governmental

purpose.  An expenditure of campaign funds is directly related to

a legislative or governmental purpose when it is [reasonably]*

foreseeable that the principal benefit of the expenditure will be

to assist in performance of the work of a legislative or

governmental body [or the official duties of the elected

officer].*  Expenditures for meals provided to the elected officer

and his or her [office]* staff at meetings, the [sole]* [primary]*

purpose of which is to confer regarding matters pertaining to the

governmental work of the elected officer, are deemed to be

directly related to a legislative or governmental purpose.

     (d) Reasonably related to a legislative or governmental

purpose.  An expenditure of campaign funds is reasonably related


_________________________

* The text appearing in brackets represents a choice which the
Commission may wish to consider.  The Commission may select the
choice, exclude the choice, or consider other choices.

8/29/91                    2                           18952

to a legislative or governmental purpose when it is [reasonably]*
foreseeable that the expenditure will [substantially]* assist in
the performance of work of a legislative or governmental body [or
the official duties of an elected officer].*

(e) This section shall not be used to determine whether
an officeholder account or an account for election to future
office shall be the source of campaign funds expended by an
elected official. Section 18525 shall be determinative of which
account may be used for expenditures of campaign funds.

AUTHORITY: Section 83112, Gov. Code.
REFERENCE: Sections 89510-89521, Gov. Code.

* The text appearing in brackets represents a choice which the
Commission may wish to consider. The Commission may select the
choice, exclude the choice, or consider other choices.

8/29/91 3 18952

sections.conclusion:
This is an extremely difficult subject in which to make regulations which are absolutely fair in every case and at the same time prevent abuses of the law. Staff has continuously sought comments from officials and their representatives so that the regulations can be made to apply in a practical and understandable manner. We continue to encourage comments so that they may be considered and possibly incorporated into the regulation before it is presented to the Commission for adoption.

ISSUE 2

When an official receives a gift from an organization, who is considered to be the source of the gift?

Current Rule: When an official receives a gift from an organization, the organization is the source of the gift and the gift limit is applied to the total value of the gift provided to the official by the organization. However, an exception exists. If the organization is acting as an intermediary, it will not be considered the "source" of the gift to the public official. Under such circumstances, the organization will be reported as an intermediary if the gift is $50 or more, and the gift limit will be applied against the actual source of the gift. (Section 87210.)

Prior Advice: Generally our prior advice has not focused upon whether an organization is the source of a gift or merely an intermediary, except in a limited way with respect to the reporting of activity expenses by lobbyist employers. (See, Bell Advice Letter, No. I-91-077.) We have advised that members of an association are generally not considered sources of gifts made by the association. (Nawi Advice Letter, No. A-84-Q13; Brockman Advice Letter, No. I-91-096.) However, we have also advised that members of an organization may be considered the source of a gift if the sole or primary purpose of their dues payment is to make gifts to public officials. (Harper Advice Letter, No. A-87-088; Brockman Advice Letter, supra.) We have advised that a nonprofit foundation could be considered an intermediary if it were merely acting as a conduit for donations received and then given to public officials. (Katz Advice Letter, No. I-90-269.) We have recently advised that a person making a donation to an organization may be considered the source of a gift to a public official if, at the time the payment is made, the person knows or had reason to know that the official was the intended recipient. (McCloskey Advice Letter, No. I-91-263.)

Concern: For purposes of the gift and honoraria provisions of the Act, the term "source" should be interpreted in a manner consistent with the declarations and purposes of the Act. Public officials, whether elected or appointed, should perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Government Code Section 81001.) Assets and income of public officials which may be materially affected by their official actions should be disclosed. (Government Code Section 81002.)

Therefore, it is important that an organization not be deemed to be the "source" of a gift when it is actually serving as an intermediary. To do so would result in incomplete disclosure to the public and would prevent the public from making an accurate assessment of possible sources of bias on the part of a public official.

Furthermore, to fail to recognize the actual source of a gift could permit a person to circumvent the gift limits by making a gift to a public official through an organization. For example, gifts of travel are generally subject to the new gift limits. (Government Code Section 89506(b).) However, gifts of travel from a charitable organization are exempt from the limits if the travel is reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose or to an issue of state, national or international public policy. (Government Code Section 89506(a)(2).)

If an individual whose gift of travel would be subject to the limits were to make that gift through a charitable organization, the individual would be able to circumvent the gift limit unless he or she were recognized as the true source of the gift. If, instead, the charitable organization is recognized as an intermediary, both the charitable organization and the actual donor will be disclosed.

Proposed Regulations 18945 and 18945.3: Staff is proposing two regulations focusing on when a person is to be considered the source of a gift and when that person should be considered an intermediary.

(1) Proposed regulation 18945 provides that a person may be the direct or indirect source of a gift. Thus, a person is the source of a gift if that person tenders a gift directly to a public official and the public official accepts the gift. A person would also be considered the source of a gift if the person tenders a payment to a third party, for example an organization, and knows or has reason to know at the time the payment is made that the payment will be used to make a gift to a public official or to an identifiable group of public officials. Dues payments to an organization would not be considered indirect gifts to persons who receive gifts from the organization paid from those dues

unless the sole or primary purpose of the dues payments was to make gifts to public officials.

(2) Proposed regulation 18945.3 clarifies that a person, for example an organization, which receives a payment from another for the purpose of making a gift to a public official or to an identifiable group of public officials is an intermediary. As an intermediary, the organization would be required to disclose to the donee not only the organization's name, address and business activity, if any, but also the name, address and business activity, if any, of the actual source of the gift. In order to avoid any misunderstanding or surprise on the part of the donee, the proposed regulation requires that the intermediary disclose this information at the time the gift is made.

Examples: In most situations, this determination of the source of a gift is easy. Generally, the "source" of a gift is going to be the person who tenders the gift to the public official. Thus, if John Doe tenders a gift to public official Y and Y accepts, John Doe is the "source" of a gift to Y. If ABC charitable organization tenders a gift to official Y and Y accepts, ABC is the "source" of a gift to Y.

Both of the foregoing examples assume that no third party was involved in the gift. That is, the presumption is that the gift to official Y was paid for by John Doe or ABC charitable organization. If this is not the case, John Doe or ABC charitable trust may not be the source of the funds. Consider the following:1

1. Suppose that Mary Smith has asked John Doe to make a gift to official Y and provides funds to John Doe for that purpose. If John Doe then uses the funds to make a gift to official Y, who should be considered the source of the gift to official Y?

Mary Smith should be considered the source of the gift. She knew or had reason to know at the time she made the payment to John Doe that the payment would be used to make a gift to official Y. John Doe should inform official Y, at the time of the gift, that Doe is an intermediary and that Smith is the source.

2. Suppose that ABC charitable organization approaches Mary Smith and says that ABC is planning to send official Y on a fact-finding trip to Europe. ABC then asks Mary Smith to make a donation to ABC to defray all or part of the costs of the trip. Who should be considered the source of the gift to official Y?

1 The following examples assume that the public official receives a gift. In many circumstances, certain payments may be income rather than gifts, may be excluded from the definition of "gift," or may be exempt from the gift limits by other provisions of the Act or by Commission regulation.

Mary Smith should be considered the source of all or a proportionate part of the gift to official Y. She knew or had reason to know at the time she made the payment to ABC that the payment would be used to make a gift to official Y. ABC should inform official Y, at the time of the gift, that ABC is an intermediary and that Smith is the source of the gift.

3. Would the analysis change if Mary Smith approached ABC charitable organization with the plan to finance official Y's trip and then made a donation to ABC organization towards those costs?

The analysis would be the same.

4. Suppose that ABC charitable organization approaches Mary Smith and says that ABC is planning to send a group of interested citizens on a fact-finding trip to Europe. If Mary Smith makes a donation to ABC organization to defray all or part of the costs of the trip, and one of the participants turns out to be official Y, who is the source of the gift to official Y?

ABC would be considered the source of the gift to official Y. At the time that Mary Smith made a donation to ABC, she did not know or have reason to know that official Y would be a donee.

5. Suppose that ABC charitable organization decides to put on a golf tournament and invite official Y. ABC approaches Mary Smith and asks for donations to pay for the event. If Mary Smith knows or has reason to know that official Y is an invitee, will Mary Smith be considered a source of all or some pro rata portion of gifts received by official Y from ABC charitable organization?

If Mary Smith knows or has reason to know that her donation towards the event will be used to make a gift to official Y, Mary Smith would be considered a source of the gift to official Y and ABC would be considered an intermediary. ABC should inform official Y, at the time of the gift, that Mary Smith is the donor and that ABC is the intermediary.

Conclusion: The proposed regulations provide a workable framework for assuring full and adequate disclosure of sources of gifts to public officials and for limiting the opportunities for circumvention of the gift limits. As a general rule, the source of a gift will be the person who tenders the gift directly to the public official. Only in certain narrow circumstances, determined at the time the initial payment is tendered, will a person be considered the indirect source of a gift. Public officials who

receive gifts are protected from undue surprise as to the source of the gift by the requirement for intermediary disclosure at the time the gift is made, by which the official must be apprised of the true source of the gift.

ISSUE 3

When must an official cumulate the value of a gift given to him by an individual with the pro rata value of a gift given by a group to which the individual has contributed?

Current Rule: An official must cumulate the value of a gift given to him by an individual with the pro rata value of a gift given by a group to which the individual has contributed either when the group is directed or controlled by the individual or when the group uses funds for the gift that are specifically contributed for the purpose of making such gifts to the official. A few examples will illustrate this rule.

Example 1: Official A receives a gift (Gift 1) from B in 1991. B then joins with C, D, and E in the same calendar year to give another gift (Gift 2) to A, and the gift is identified as being from B, C, D, and E. Under these circumstances A must cumulate the value of Gift 1 with B's pro rata share of Gift 2.

Example 2: Official A receives a gift (Gift 1) from B in 1991. B then joins with C, D, and E, from a group called "Good Government People," and the group collects funds from B, C, D, and E to give a gift (Gift 2) to A in the same calendar year. Since the "Good Government People" collected funds specifically to make a gift to A, A must cumulate the value of Gift 1 with B's pro rata share of Gift 2. Government Code Section 87210 requires that an intermediary making a gift to an official disclose both his own full name, street address, and business activity if any, and the full name, street address, and business activity if any of the actual donor. Under the above circumstances if "Good Government People" does not disclose that Gift 2 is from B, C, D, and E, A is under no obligation to cumulate B's pro rata share of Gift 2 with Gift 1. However, if A knew, or should have known that "Good Government People" consisted of B and some other persons, or that B was connected to "Good Government People," A would have an obligation to inquire whether Gift 2 was attributed in whole, or in part, to B.

If A discovers at some future date that Gift 2 was attributable to B, A must determine the pro rata share of Gift 2 attributable to B and cumulate with the value of Gift 1. If this cumulative value exceeds $250, A will not be deemed to have violated the gift limits if A returns the portion of the value of the gift in excess of $250 within 30 days of the date that A discovers that Gift 2 was attributable to B. (Regulation 18726.1; Duffy Advice Letter, No. A-84-84.)

Example 3: Official A receives a gift (Gift 1) from B in 1991. B is a wine producer and is a member of the "California Wine Institute" (the "Wine Institute"). The Wine Institute gives a gift (Gift 2) to A in the same calendar year.

Under these circumstances, the gift is from the Wine Institute and A does not have to cumulate the value of Gift 1 with B's pro rata share of Gift 2 merely because B is a member of the Wine Institute. If B and some other members of the Wine Institute specifically provided funds for purposes of making Gift 2 to A, then Wine Institute would be an intermediary and would be required to disclose to A in addition to its own name, street, and business activity, but also the names, street address, and business activities of the individual contributors to Gift 2. A must then cumulate the value of Gift 1 with B's pro rata share of Gift 2.

Concerns: Concerns have been raised that an official who receives a gift from a group may not be aware that a member of the group has previously given a gift as an individual and the official may exceed the gift limits imposed by the Act and may thereby inadvertently violate the law. However, as the above examples indicate, the official must cumulate when the official knows, or should have known that a gift from a group is attributable, in whole or in part, to an individual who has previously given a gift. If the official determines at a later date that a gift from a group was in fact attributable in part to an individual who had previously given a gift and the cumulative value of gifts from that individual exceeded the limits, the official should, within 30 days of the date he or she discovers this fact, return to or reimburse, the individual for that portion of the gift in excess of the limits.

Conclusion: Because of the question of knowledge of the official, this issue does not lend itself to an easy solution by regulation. However, staff is prepared to work with legislative staff to address any perceived problems.

ISSUE 4

What types of expenditures are "directly related to a political purpose" under the Act's "personal use" provisions?
sections.facts:
Prior to 1990, the "personal use" law was in the Elections Code and under the aegis of the Attorney General. At that time, and now, the basis for determining the permissibility of an expenditure of campaign funds was the degree of relation between the expenditure and a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. However, when the Legislature incorporated personal use into the Political Reform Act (as of January 1, 1990), it also significantly altered the applicable standards for the permissible expenditure of campaign funds, thereby substantially raising the acceptable thresholds.

Specifically, prior law allowed an expenditure of campaign funds that had "more than a negligible political, legislative, or governmental purpose" even when personal benefit accrued to an elected officer from the expenditure. Expenditures in certain categories were required to meet a higher standard, that of a "reasonable relation" to the required purposes.

Current Rule: The current law, raised the basic standard for expenditure of campaign funds from the lower "more than a negligible relation" standard that constituted prior law to the level of the former upper tier, the "reasonably related" level. Further, where a candidate or elected officer receives a substantial personal benefit (defined as a direct personal benefit valued at over $100) from the expenditure of campaign funds, a new and significantly narrower standard, the "directly related" standard, has been expressly mandated by the Legislature.

In other words, expenditures of campaign funds must now, at a minimum, be "reasonably related" to a political, legislative or governmental purpose. And if the expenditure confers a direct personal benefit of over $100 on the elected officer or candidate, that expenditure must be "directly related" to a political, legislative or legislative purpose. Also, it is important to note that several categories of campaign expenditures, such as purchases of tickets to sporting events and personal gifts, are required to satisfy the "directly related" standard, regardless of whether personal benefit is obtained. (See Government Code Section 89513.)

Proposed Regulation: The definitional language proposed in new Regulation 18952 for "directly related to a political purpose" requires that an expenditure of campaign funds be "necessary," "inherent," or "inextricably linked" to a political purpose. These are narrow parameters, to be sure, but the terms do reflect the alterations enacted by the Legislature. Staff recognizes, after several months of grappling with the issue of personal use definitions, that terms such as "directly related" and "reasonably related" do not lend themselves to precise definition; but it was felt that the terms are so basic to the personal use statutes that it was important to give guidance regarding the meaning of the phrases on which the entire framework of personal use is based.

The proposed definitional regulation recognizes that certain activities, such as fundraising and activities to inform voters of the record and viewpoints of a candidate, are fundamental to the political process. Consequently, a list of activities, deemed to be "directly related to a political purpose" is included, for purposes of practicality, in the proposed regulation. If a candidate were to expend funds for any of the listed activities which encompass the political campaign activity spectrum, there would be no need to justify the expenditure as "directly related."

Conclusion: A copy of the proposed regulation is enclosed for your review; I think a reading of the proposed regulatory language will indicate that the list of directly related activities is workable, in that it incorporates necessary campaign activities while taking into consideration the current legislatively-mandated statutory strictures.

SH:aa:gifts1

Adopt 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18952 to read:

18952. Definitions of Directly Related to a Political Purpose, Reasonably Related to a Political Purpose, Directly Related to a Legislative or Governmental Purpose, and Reasonably Related to a Legislative or Governmental Purpose

The following definitions shall pertain to any expenditure of campaign funds by a candidate or elected officer pursuant to Government Code Sections 89510-89521.

(a) Directly related to a political purpose. [An expenditure of campaign funds by a candidate or a candidate controlled committee is directly related to a political purpose if the expenditure is [necessary]* [inherent]* [inextricably linked]* to the candidate's quest for elective office.]* Expenditures deemed to be directly related to a political purpose include [but are not limited to]*:

(1) Expenditures in connection with fundraising for the candidate's campaign for elective office.

(2) Expenditures for meals provided to the candidate, campaign staff members, and contributors at meetings, the [sole]* purpose of which is to discuss campaign issues or strategy.

(3) Expenditures in connection with programs or events to inform voters as to [the past record or current position]* [views]* of the candidate on campaign issues.

* The text appearing in brackets represents a choice which the Commission may wish to consider. The Commission may select the choice, exclude the choice, or consider other choices.

(4) Expenses for get-out-the-vote promotional mailings and get-out-the-vote events in the candidate's jurisdiction.

(5) Expenses incurred for operation of the candidate's controlled committee [in connection with performance of the administrative functions of the committee].*

(b) Reasonably related to a political purpose. An expenditure of campaign funds is reasonably related to a political purpose when it is [reasonably]* foreseeable that the expenditure would further the quest for elective office of the candidate expending the funds.

(c) Directly related to a legislative or governmental purpose. An expenditure of campaign funds is directly related to a legislative or governmental purpose when it is [reasonably]* foreseeable that the principal benefit of the expenditure will be to assist in performance of the work of a legislative or governmental body [or the official duties of the elected officer].* Expenditures for meals provided to the elected officer and his or her [office]* staff at meetings, the [sole]* [primary]* purpose of which is to confer regarding matters pertaining to the governmental work of the elected officer, are deemed to be directly related to a legislative or governmental purpose.

(d) Reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose. An expenditure of campaign funds is reasonably related

* The text appearing in brackets represents a choice which the Commission may wish to consider. The Commission may select the choice, exclude the choice, or consider other choices.

to a legislative or governmental purpose when it is [reasonably]* foreseeable that the expenditure will [substantially]* assist in the performance of work of a legislative or governmental body [or the official duties of an elected officer].*

(e) This section shall not be used to determine whether an officeholder account or an account for election to future office shall be the source of campaign funds expended by an elected official. Section 18525 shall be determinative of which account may be used for expenditures of campaign funds.

AUTHORITY: Section 83112, Gov. Code.
REFERENCE: Sections 89510-89521, Gov. Code.

* The text appearing in brackets represents a choice which the Commission may wish to consider. The Commission may select the choice, exclude the choice, or consider other choices.

embedding.qa_text:
CONCLUSION: This is an extremely difficult subject in which to make regulations which are absolutely fair in every case and at the same time prevent abuses of the law. Staff has continuously sought comments from officials and their representatives so that the regulations can be made to apply in a practical and understandable manner. We continue to encourage comments so that they may be considered and possibly incorporated into the regulation before it is presented to the Commission for adoption.

ISSUE 2

When an official receives a gift from an organization, who is considered to be the source of the gift?

Current Rule: When an official receives a gift from an organization, the organization is the source of the gift and the gift limit is applied to the total value of the gift provided to the official by the organization. However, an exception exists. If the organization is acting as an intermediary, it will not be considered the "source" of the gift to the public official. Under such circumstances, the organization will be reported as an intermediary if the gift is $50 or more, and the gift limit will be applied against the actual source of the gift. (Section 87210.)

Prior Advice: Generally our prior advice has not focused upon whether an organization is the source of a gift or merely an intermediary, except in a limited way with respect to the reporting of activity expenses by lobbyist employers. (See, Bell Advice Letter, No. I-91-077.) We have advised that members of an association are generally not considered sources of gifts made by the association. (Nawi Advice Letter, No. A-84-Q13; Brockman Advice Letter, No. I-91-096.) However, we have also advised that members of an organization may be considered the source of a gift if the sole or primary purpose of their dues payment is to make gifts to public officials. (Harper Advice Letter, No. A-87-088; Brockman Advice Letter, supra.) We have advised that a nonprofit foundation could be considered an intermediary if it were merely acting as a conduit for donations received and then given to public officials. (Katz Advice Letter, No. I-90-269.) We have recently advised that a person making a donation to an organization may be considered the source of a gift to a public official if, at the time the payment is made, the person knows or had reason to know that the official was the intended recipient. (McCloskey Advice Letter, No. I-91-263.)

Concern: For purposes of the gift and honoraria provisions of the Act, the term "source" should be interpreted in a manner consistent with the declarations and purposes of the Act. Public officials, whether elected or appointed, should perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Government Code Section 81001.) Assets and income of public officials which may be materially affected by their official actions should be disclosed. (Government Code Section 81002.)

Therefore, it is important that an organization not be deemed to be the "source" of a gift when it is actually serving as an intermediary. To do so would result in incomplete disclosure to the public and would prevent the public from making an accurate assessment of possible sources of bias on the part of a public official.

Furthermore, to fail to recognize the actual source of a gift could permit a person to circumvent the gift limits by making a gift to a public official through an organization. For example, gifts of travel are generally subject to the new gift limits. (Government Code Section 89506(b).) However, gifts of travel from a charitable organization are exempt from the limits if the travel is reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose or to an issue of state, national or international public policy. (Government Code Section 89506(a)(2).)

If an individual whose gift of travel would be subject to the limits were to make that gift through a charitable organization, the individual would be able to circumvent the gift limit unless he or she were recognized as the true source of the gift. If, instead, the charitable organization is recognized as an intermediary, both the charitable organization and the actual donor will be disclosed.

Proposed Regulations 18945 and 18945.3: Staff is proposing two regulations focusing on when a person is to be considered the source of a gift and when that person should be considered an intermediary.

(1) Proposed regulation 18945 provides that a person may be the direct or indirect source of a gift. Thus, a person is the source of a gift if that person tenders a gift directly to a public official and the public official accepts the gift. A person would also be considered the source of a gift if the person tenders a payment to a third party, for example an organization, and knows or has reason to know at the time the payment is made that the payment will be used to make a gift to a public official or to an identifiable group of public officials. Dues payments to an organization would not be considered indirect gifts to persons who receive gifts from the organization paid from those dues

unless the sole or primary purpose of the dues payments was to make gifts to public officials.

(2) Proposed regulation 18945.3 clarifies that a person, for example an organization, which receives a payment from another for the purpose of making a gift to a public official or to an identifiable group of public officials is an intermediary. As an intermediary, the organization would be required to disclose to the donee not only the organization's name, address and business activity, if any, but also the name, address and business activity, if any, of the actual source of the gift. In order to avoid any misunderstanding or surprise on the part of the donee, the proposed regulation requires that the intermediary disclose this information at the time the gift is made.

Examples: In most situations, this determination of the source of a gift is easy. Generally, the "source" of a gift is going to be the person who tenders the gift to the public official. Thus, if John Doe tenders a gift to public official Y and Y accepts, John Doe is the "source" of a gift to Y. If ABC charitable organization tenders a gift to official Y and Y accepts, ABC is the "source" of a gift to Y.

Both of the foregoing examples assume that no third party was involved in the gift. That is, the presumption is that the gift to official Y was paid for by John Doe or ABC charitable organization. If this is not the case, John Doe or ABC charitable trust may not be the source of the funds. Consider the following:1

1. Suppose that Mary Smith has asked John Doe to make a gift to official Y and provides funds to John Doe for that purpose. If John Doe then uses the funds to make a gift to official Y, who should be considered the source of the gift to official Y?

Mary Smith should be considered the source of the gift. She knew or had reason to know at the time she made the payment to John Doe that the payment would be used to make a gift to official Y. John Doe should inform official Y, at the time of the gift, that Doe is an intermediary and that Smith is the source.

2. Suppose that ABC charitable organization approaches Mary Smith and says that ABC is planning to send official Y on a fact-finding trip to Europe. ABC then asks Mary Smith to make a donation to ABC to defray all or part of the costs of the trip. Who should be considered the source of the gift to official Y?

1 The following examples assume that the public official receives a gift. In many circumstances, certain payments may be income rather than gifts, may be excluded from the definition of "gift," or may be exempt from the gift limits by other provisions of the Act or by Commission regulation.

Mary Smith should be considered the source of all or a proportionate part of the gift to official Y. She knew or had reason to know at the time she made the payment to ABC that the payment would be used to make a gift to official Y. ABC should inform official Y, at the time of the gift, that ABC is an intermediary and that Smith is the source of the gift.

3. Would the analysis change if Mary Smith approached ABC charitable organization with the plan to finance official Y's trip and then made a donation to ABC organization towards those costs?

The analysis would be the same.

4. Suppose that ABC charitable organization approaches Mary Smith and says that ABC is planning to send a group of interested citizens on a fact-finding trip to Europe. If Mary Smith makes a donation to ABC organization to defray all or part of the costs of the trip, and one of the participants turns out to be official Y, who is the source of the gift to official Y?

ABC would be considered the source of the gift to official Y. At the time that Mary Smith made a donation to ABC, she did not know or have reason to know that official Y would be a donee.

5. Suppose that ABC charitable organization decides to put on a golf tournament and invite official Y. ABC approaches Mary Smith and asks for donations to pay for the event. If Mary Smith knows or has reason to know that official Y is an invitee, will Mary Smith be considered a source of all or some pro rata portion of gifts received by official Y from ABC charitable organization?

If Mary Smith knows or has reason to know that her donation towards the event will be used to make a gift to official Y, Mary Smith would be considered a source of the gift to official Y and ABC would be considered an intermediary. ABC should inform official Y, at the time of the gift, that Mary Smith is the donor and that ABC is the intermediary.

Conclusion: The proposed regulations provide a workable framework for assuring full and adequate disclosure of sources of gifts to public officials and for limiting the opportunities for circumvention of the gift limits. As a general rule, the source of a gift will be the person who tenders the gift directly to the public official. Only in certain narrow circumstances, determined at the time the initial payment is tendered, will a person be considered the indirect source of a gift. Public officials who

receive gifts are protected from undue surprise as to the source of the gift by the requirement for intermediary disclosure at the time the gift is made, by which the official must be apprised of the true source of the gift.

ISSUE 3

When must an official cumulate the value of a gift given to him by an individual with the pro rata value of a gift given by a group to which the individual has contributed?

Current Rule: An official must cumulate the value of a gift given to him by an individual with the pro rata value of a gift given by a group to which the individual has contributed either when the group is directed or controlled by the individual or when the group uses funds for the gift that are specifically contributed for the purpose of making such gifts to the official. A few examples will illustrate this rule.

Example 1: Official A receives a gift (Gift 1) from B in 1991. B then joins with C, D, and E in the same calendar year to give another gift (Gift 2) to A, and the gift is identified as being from B, C, D, and E. Under these circumstances A must cumulate the value of Gift 1 with B's pro rata share of Gift 2.

Example 2: Official A receives a gift (Gift 1) from B in 1991. B then joins with C, D, and E, from a group called "Good Government People," and the group collects funds from B, C, D, and E to give a gift (Gift 2) to A in the same calendar year. Since the "Good Government People" collected funds specifically to make a gift to A, A must cumulate the value of Gift 1 with B's pro rata share of Gift 2. Government Code Section 87210 requires that an intermediary making a gift to an official disclose both his own full name, street address, and business activity if any, and the full name, street address, and business activity if any of the actual donor. Under the above circumstances if "Good Government People" does not disclose that Gift 2 is from B, C, D, and E, A is under no obligation to cumulate B's pro rata share of Gift 2 with Gift 1. However, if A knew, or should have known that "Good Government People" consisted of B and some other persons, or that B was connected to "Good Government People," A would have an obligation to inquire whether Gift 2 was attributed in whole, or in part, to B.

If A discovers at some future date that Gift 2 was attributable to B, A must determine the pro rata share of Gift 2 attributable to B and cumulate with the value of Gift 1. If this cumulative value exceeds $250, A will not be deemed to have violated the gift limits if A returns the portion of the value of the gift in excess of $250 within 30 days of the date that A discovers that Gift 2 was attributable to B. (Regulation 18726.1; Duffy Advice Letter, No. A-84-84.)

Example 3: Official A receives a gift (Gift 1) from B in 1991. B is a wine producer and is a member of the "California Wine Institute" (the "Wine Institute"). The Wine Institute gives a gift (Gift 2) to A in the same calendar year.

Under these circumstances, the gift is from the Wine Institute and A does not have to cumulate the value of Gift 1 with B's pro rata share of Gift 2 merely because B is a member of the Wine Institute. If B and some other members of the Wine Institute specifically provided funds for purposes of making Gift 2 to A, then Wine Institute would be an intermediary and would be required to disclose to A in addition to its own name, street, and business activity, but also the names, street address, and business activities of the individual contributors to Gift 2. A must then cumulate the value of Gift 1 with B's pro rata share of Gift 2.

Concerns: Concerns have been raised that an official who receives a gift from a group may not be aware that a member of the group has previously given a gift as an individual and the official may exceed the gift limits imposed by the Act and may thereby inadvertently violate the law. However, as the above examples indicate, the official must cumulate when the official knows, or should have known that a gift from a group is attributable, in whole or in part, to an individual who has previously given a gift. If the official determines at a later date that a gift from a group was in fact attributable in part to an individual who had previously given a gift and the cumulative value of gifts from that individual exceeded the limits, the official should, within 30 days of the date he or she discovers this fact, return to or reimburse, the individual for that portion of the gift in excess of the limits.

Conclusion: Because of the question of knowledge of the official, this issue does not lend itself to an easy solution by regulation. However, staff is prepared to work with legislative staff to address any perceived problems.

ISSUE 4

What types of expenditures are "directly related to a political purpose" under the Act's "personal use" provisions?
embedding.first_500_words: **Memorandum** To: Chairman Davidian Date: September 17, 1991 From: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Subject: Issues Concerning Gifts At your request the Legal Division has analyzed four general issues concerning gifts to public officials. We understand that these issues have been raised by some members of the Legislature. Each issue, and its respective analysis, is set forth below. ISSUE 1 Has an official received a gift when a gift is given to the official’s spouse or immediate family member? If the official has received a gift, what is its value? Current Rule: Commission Regulation 18726.2 provides the existing rule regarding these types of gifts. It addresses two situations: (1) The gift is given directly to a member of the official’s immediate family. Generally, in this situation there is no gift to the official unless he or she uses the gift, disposes of the gift or enjoys a direct benefit from the gift. If this occurs, the official has received the gift and the full value is attributed to the official. By advice letter, the Commission has created an exception to this rule where the gift is given to the official’s spouse and the circumstances show that the donor in no way intended to benefit the official. In that situation, there is no gift to the official even though he or she may derive some benefit from the gift. Example: Old family friend of the official and his spouse sends a letter to the spouse saying: "Enclosed are the keys to my mobilehome. Please feel free to use it any time." If the official and spouse use the mobilehome, it is a gift in its entirety to the official based on the reasonable value of the use. Example: Same situation, but the gift is from the spouse’s employer, who does not know the official and is offering use of the mobilehome as a reward for the spouse's work at the office. This is not a gift to the official even if the official uses the mobilehome. (2) The gift is given to the official and designated for use by the official and his or her spouse or family. Here, there is a gift in its entirety to the official only where he or she exercises control over who actually receives the gift or if he or she receives a direct benefit from the gift. Example: Old family friend gives official an authentic replica of the Liberty Bell on the Fourth of July, saying, "Here's a little gift for you and your wife." This would be a gift in its entirety to the official because he is receiving a direct benefit from the gift. Example: Corporation sends official a letter with two airline tickets and hotel reservations for two enclosed. The letter reads: "Dear Official: Here are airline tickets and hotel reservations for you and your spouse to spend one night at our annual convention in Cooperstown, N.Y. You have agreed to address our convention on the value of professional baseball to the
